tasks.title: Audit and fix guild quest assignment — can the player get quests from guild masters?
tasks.description: CRITICAL: Verify that the player can walk into any guild hall (Merchants, Artisans, Scholars, Explorers, Performers), talk to a guild master NPC, and receive guild quests in the correct order. Currently GuildQuestManager (shared/quests/guild-quest-manager.ts) has getNextQuestForGuild(), receiveNextQuest(), getJoinQuest(), and hasJoinedGuild() methods, but guild master NPCs may not be spawned or identifiable in the game world. (1) Verify that each guild building in the settlement has at least one NPC with the 'guildMaster' role or occupation matching the guild (e.g., Blacksmith for Artisans guild). Check shared/guild-definitions.ts for guildMasterOccupation fields and confirm these NPCs exist in the world data for the test world. If guild master NPCs are missing, add logic to BabylonGame.ts or the world generation pipeline to ensure each guild building always has a guild master NPC spawned inside. (2) Verify the guild quest queue: when the player talks to a guild master, the conversation system should check GuildQuestManager.hasJoinedGuild() — if false, offer the Tier 0 join quest; if true, offer the next quest via getNextQuestForGuild(). Only ONE guild quest per guild should be active at a time. Check if this gating logic exists in the chat panel's quest offering flow (BabylonGame.ts ~line 2739 onQuestAssigned / chatPanel setOnQuestAssigned). (3) Verify quest progression: when a guild quest completes, the next quest in the guild chain becomes available. Check that quest completion events properly update the guild tier and unlock the next quest. (4) Add a test that loads the test world, identifies all guild buildings, confirms each has a guild master NPC, and confirms the join quest is retrievable for each guild. (5) If guild master NPCs are not being spawned inside guild interiors, wire InteriorNPCManager to always place the guild master at a prominent position (behind a desk/counter) when the player enters a guild building.

tasks.title: Audit and fix crafting system end-to-end — can the player actually craft items?
tasks.description: CRITICAL: The Artisans guild has 4 quests with craft_item objectives (Première Création, Suivre les Instructions, Chef-d'Œuvre, L'Herboriste) but the player may have no way to actually reach a crafting station or open a crafting UI. Two separate crafting systems exist: RecipeCraftingSystem (shared/game-engine/logic/RecipeCraftingSystem.ts) with 10 French-themed recipes across kitchen_stove/alchemy_table/workbench stations, and CraftingSystem (shared/game-engine/logic/CraftingSystem.ts) which BabylonGame actually initializes at ~line 15997. (1) Determine which CraftingSystem is actually used by BabylonGame and whether the RecipeCraftingSystem is dead code or the intended replacement. Check if BabylonGame.ts imports both and which one's methods are called. (2) Check if a crafting UI panel exists — search for BabylonCraftingPanel, CraftingPanel, or any rendering component that shows recipes. If NO crafting panel UI exists, create one following the BabylonShopPanel pattern: show available recipes on the left, player inventory materials on the right, a 'Craft' button, a progress bar during crafting, and the result item with French/English vocabulary display. (3) Add 'GuildArtisans' to the workbench station's buildingTypes array in RecipeCraftingSystem.ts CRAFTING_STATIONS (currently only ['Blacksmith', 'Carpenter', 'Warehouse']). (4) Check if crafting stations are placed as action hotspots inside guild/workshop interiors. If not, wire PlayerActionSystem.getHotspotsForBusiness() to return crafting hotspots for GuildArtisans, Blacksmith, and Carpenter business types, and ensure the InteractionPromptSystem shows '[G]: Craft' when near them. (5) Verify the item_crafted event flows from CraftingSystem → GameEventBus → QuestCompletionEngine.trackItemCrafted(). (6) Test: enter an Artisans guild, approach a workbench/crafting station, open the crafting UI, craft an item, confirm the craft_item quest objective updates.

tasks.title: Audit item usage and placement — can the player find, use, equip, and consume items?
tasks.description: Verify the complete item lifecycle: items exist in the world → player can find/collect them → items appear in inventory → player can use/equip/consume them → usage triggers events that update quest state. (1) Check how items are placed in the world: ContainerSpawnSystem.ts spawns loot containers (chests, barrels, crates) with context-sensitive items based on business type. Verify that containers actually appear in settlements and interiors, contain items, and that clicking them opens a loot panel. (2) Check what happens when the player presses 'Use' on an inventory item: BabylonInventory.ts has onItemUsed, onItemEquipped, onItemUnequipped callbacks (lines 100-102) and shows Use/Read buttons for consumable, food, drink, quest, key, document items. Verify BabylonGame.ts registers these callbacks and that using an item has visible effects (health restore for food, energy restore for drinks, quest progress for quest items). Check BabylonGame.ts handleUseItem / handleEquipItem flows. (3) Verify item consumption: when a food/drink item is used, it should be removed from inventory (quantity decremented) and apply effects (heal HP, restore energy). Check if this actually happens or if the callback is a no-op. (4) Verify equipment: when a weapon/armor is equipped, it should appear in the equipment slot, apply stat bonuses (ATK/DEF), and be visible on the character if possible. (5) Check which items are part of quests (collect_item, deliver_item, craft_item objectives) and confirm those items can actually be found in the world. Are quest-required items injected into containers via QuestItemObjective? (6) Verify item events: item_used, item_equipped, item_collected, item_delivered events must flow through GameEventBus → QuestCompletionEngine. (7) Check if the inventory opens (I key or tab) and displays correctly with all item categories.

tasks.title: Audit watch/observe quests — does observing NPCs properly complete objectives?
tasks.description: Verify the full observe/watch pipeline: player sees an NPC performing an activity → observation is detected → event is emitted → quest objective updates. (1) Check NPCActivityLabelSystem (shared/game-engine/rendering/NPCActivityLabelSystem.ts): does it detect when the player is looking at an NPC performing a specific activity (cooking, working, farming, fishing, etc.)? How does it determine the player is 'watching'? Is it proximity-based, gaze-based, or time-based? (2) Check ActivityObservationRewards (shared/game-engine/logic/ActivityObservationRewards.ts): does it show a toast and teach vocabulary when the player observes an activity? Is there a minimum observation duration (observeDurationRequired) before it counts? (3) Verify the event chain: NPCActivityLabelSystem detects observation → emits activity_observed event on GameEventBus → BabylonGame bridge at ~line 2255 calls engine.trackEvent({ type: 'activity_observed', npcId, npcName, activity, durationSeconds }) → QuestCompletionEngine.trackActivityObserved() matches against observe_activity objectives. (4) Check if NPCs actually perform visible activities: do occupation-based animations play (baker kneading, blacksmith hammering, fisherman casting)? If NPCs just stand idle, observation quests are impossible. Check OccupationActivitySystem.ts and BusinessBehaviorSystem for activity animation wiring. (5) Test the eavesdrop system: QuestCompletionEngine has an 'eavesdrop' case (line 432). Verify BabylonChatPanel's eavesdrop mode works — can the player listen to NPC-to-NPC conversations and have it count toward objectives? (6) Add duration feedback: show a progress indicator when the player is observing (e.g., 'Observing... 3/5 seconds') so they know when the observation will count.

tasks.title: Audit action hotspots — can the player perform special actions at hotspot locations?
tasks.description: Verify that action hotspots (fishing, mining, herbalism, cooking, crafting, chopping, painting, farming) are generated, placed in the world, interactable, and emit events that update quest state. (1) Check ActionHotspotGenerator (shared/game-engine/logic/ActionHotspotGenerator.ts): it defines hotspot types with matchCondition functions tied to location types and business types. Verify it's actually called during world setup in BabylonGame.ts — search for ActionHotspotGenerator instantiation and generateHotspots() calls. If it's defined but never called, wire it into the settlement generation flow. (2) Check if hotspot meshes are placed in the 3D world: hotspots need visible indicators (particles, ground markers, glowing spots) at their positions. The ActionHotspot interface has indicatorType ('particle' | 'ground_marker' | 'glow'). Check if any of these are rendered. If not, create hotspot indicator meshes during world setup. (3) Check InteractionPromptSystem: does it register action hotspots and show '[G]: Fish' / '[G]: Mine' / '[G]: Harvest' prompts when the player is near? BabylonGame.ts has registerActionHotspot calls at ~lines 9436-9445 for interior hotspots — verify outdoor hotspots are also registered. (4) Check PlayerActionSystem (shared/game-engine/rendering/PlayerActionSystem.ts): it defines 13+ action types with animations, durations, energy costs, XP rewards, and item rewards. When the player presses G at a hotspot, does the action actually execute? Is there a progress bar? Does the player receive items? (5) Verify event emission: physical_action_completed events must flow through GameEventBus → QuestCompletionEngine.trackPhysicalAction() for physical_action quest objectives. (6) Test each action type: fishing at water, mining at rocks, herbalism at gardens/forests, cooking at kitchen, crafting at workbench. Confirm items are awarded and events fire.

tasks.title: Audit and implement farming system — can the player plant, water, and harvest crops?
tasks.description: Farming actions (farm_plant, farm_water, farm_harvest) are defined in PlayerActionSystem.ts but no dedicated farming system exists. Multiple quests reference farming activities (L'Herboriste in Artisans guild, plus radiant quests). (1) Check if farming plots/gardens are generated anywhere near settlements. Search for farm plot generation in the settlement/world generation code. If no farming plots exist, create a FarmPlotGenerator that places 1-2 farming plots on the outskirts of each settlement (near Farm businesses if they exist, otherwise at a random edge location). Each plot should be a visible rectangular area (6x4 units) with tilled soil texture and a boundary fence/border. (2) Check if farm_plant, farm_water, farm_harvest actions in PlayerActionSystem actually work. They need: a planting action that consumes a seed item and creates a crop state, a watering action that advances crop growth, and a harvesting action that gives the player the crop item. If these are just action definitions with no state tracking, implement a lightweight FarmingSystem: track crop states per plot (empty → planted → growing → ready → harvested), advance growth on water actions, award harvest items. (3) Place farming hotspots on the farming plots with appropriate interaction prompts ('[G]: Plant', '[G]: Water', '[G]: Harvest' based on crop state). (4) Ensure farming actions emit physical_action_completed events so farming quest objectives update. (5) Add farming vocabulary integration: each farming action should teach/reinforce target-language vocabulary (planter, arroser, récolter for French). (6) Add quest waypoint markers: farming quest objectives should show a marker on the minimap pointing to the nearest farming plot so players can find it.

tasks.title: Audit main quest E2E — can the Missing Writer mystery be completed start to finish?
tasks.description: The Missing Writer main quest is a 6-chapter narrative (main-quest-chain-seeder.ts, main-quest-chapters.ts) that should be completable from beginning to end. Walk through every chapter and verify each objective is achievable. (1) Chapter 1 'Assignment Abroad' (A1): player arrives, visits newspaper office, learns basic greetings, gets first lead. Verify: does the newspaper office exist as a building? Is there an NPC to talk to? Does the conversation objective complete when the player talks to them? Can the player actually learn the greetings required? (2) Chapter 2 'Following the Trail' (A1): player explores, talks to locals who knew the writer, collects the writer's first book. Verify: are the 'locals who knew the writer' identifiable NPCs? Does the book exist as a collectible text in the world? Does collecting it fire text_collected → QCE? (3) Chapter 3 'The Inner Circle' (A2): befriend writer's associates, discover controversy, collect documents. Verify: friendship/relationship objectives — does the npc_relationship_changed bridge work? Can documents be found and collected? (4) Chapter 4 'Hidden Messages' (A2): discover coded messages in books, follow clues to other locations. Verify: are comprehension questions tied to quest objectives? Does answering them fire comprehension_answer events? (5) Chapter 5 'The Truth Emerges' (B1): piece together mystery, debate with scholars. Verify: conversation_goal_evaluated events from ConversationQuestBridge fire for complex conversation objectives. (6) Chapter 6 'The Final Chapter' (B2): locate writer, culminating conversation, file report. Verify: writing_submitted events work for the report objective. (7) Identify the shortest critical path: what is the minimum set of actions to complete the main quest? List each step. (8) Verify chapter unlocking: does completing Chapter N unlock Chapter N+1? Is CEFR gating working? (9) Check that the quest journal (J key) shows main quest progress distinctly from side quests.

tasks.title: Audit journal and case notes — do they update with main quest progress?
tasks.description: The Missing Writer investigation should have a case notes / journal system that records the player's discoveries and progress. ClueStore (shared/game-engine/logic/ClueStore.ts) manages investigation clues with categories (witness_testimony, written_evidence, physical_evidence, photo_evidence) and chapter gating. (1) Verify ClueStore is instantiated in BabylonGame.ts and connected to the GameEventBus. Check if 'clue_discovered' events are emitted when the player finds evidence. (2) Check if there's a UI to VIEW case notes / clues. Search for a CluePanel, CaseNotesPanel, or JournalPanel in the rendering directory. If no UI exists, the player has no way to review their investigation progress — this needs to be built. At minimum, add a 'Case Notes' tab to the GameMenuSystem (J key menu) that shows discovered clues organized by chapter, with categories color-coded (witness = blue, written = yellow, physical = green, photo = purple). (3) Verify clue discovery triggers: when the player completes a main quest objective that reveals information (talking to a witness, collecting a document, reading a book, taking a photo), a clue should be added to ClueStore. Check if these triggers exist in the quest completion flow. (4) Verify chapter gating: ClueStore.setAllowedChapters() should prevent clues from later chapters from appearing early. Check if this is called when chapters unlock. (5) Check if the quest journal (J key / GameMenuSystem) shows quest descriptions, objective checklists, and completion status. Verify it updates in real-time as objectives complete — not just on menu open. (6) Add a 'New Clue!' notification when a clue is discovered, with a brief toast and a persistent indicator on the journal button showing unread clues.

tasks.title: Audit NPC conversation initiation — do NPCs proactively approach and talk to the player?
tasks.description: NPCs should occasionally approach the player to initiate conversation — about quests, to offer radiant quests, or just for ambient dialogue. NPCInitiatedConversationController (shared/game-engine/rendering/NPCInitiatedConversationController.ts) exists with personality-driven approach evaluation, but it may not be fully functional. (1) Verify NPCInitiatedConversationController is initialized in BabylonGame.ts (~line 4282) and its callbacks (onMoveTo, onFaceDirection, onAnimationChange, onShowGreeting, onOpenChat) are wired to actual game systems. (2) Test the approach evaluation: do NPCs evaluate proximity + personality traits (extroversion, agreeableness) and actually walk toward the player? Or do they just stand there? Check if the movement command from onMoveTo actually triggers NPC pathfinding toward the player position. (3) Check the greeting prompt: when an NPC approaches, does a UI prompt appear saying 'NPC Name wants to talk [G to accept]'? If the player accepts, does the chat panel open with the NPC speaking first? (4) Check frequency tuning: NPCs should initiate about once every 5-10 real minutes of gameplay, not constantly (annoying) and not never (dead world). Check the cooldown/timer settings. Personality should matter — extroverted NPCs approach more often, introverted ones rarely. (5) Check quest-related initiation: if an NPC has a radiant quest to offer or is relevant to the player's current quest, they should have a higher approach probability. Is there integration between the quest system and the approach evaluator? (6) Verify that when the player approaches an NPC and opens conversation (not NPC-initiated), the NPC responds immediately with a contextual greeting — not silence. Check if the chat panel sends an initial NPC greeting message on conversation open. (7) Check AmbientConversationSystem: do NPC-to-NPC ambient conversations happen? Can the player observe/eavesdrop on them?

tasks.title: Audit conversation action registration — do conversation actions complete quest objectives?
tasks.description: Conversation-based quest objectives (talk_to_npc, introduce_self, ask_for_directions, order_food, haggle_price, use_vocabulary, complete_conversation, navigate_language) are the largest category of quest objectives (60+ objectives, 27% of all quests). The ConversationQuestBridge was recently built to evaluate these via the metadata response. (1) Verify ConversationQuestBridge (shared/game-engine/logic/ConversationQuestBridge.ts) is instantiated in BabylonGame.ts, has its questTracker and eventBus set, and is passed to chatPanel.setQuestBridge(). (2) Verify the metadata request includes active objectives: in BabylonChatPanel.ts, the requestMetadata() call should include questBridge.getObjectivesForEvaluation(currentNpcId) as activeObjectives. Check that the server's metadata endpoint (server/services/conversation/http-bridge.ts) receives these objectives and runs the parallel LLM evaluation via buildConversationGoalPrompt(). (3) Verify the evaluation response flows back: metadata.goalEvaluations should be processed by questBridge.processEvaluations(), which fires conversation_goal_evaluated events to QuestCompletionEngine. (4) Test each conversation objective type: start a quest with a talk_to_npc objective, have the conversation, and verify the objective completes. Then test introduce_self (player introduces themselves in target language), ask_for_directions, order_food, haggle_price. (5) Verify the confidence threshold: processEvaluations() only accepts evaluations with goalMet=true and confidence >= 0.7. Is 0.7 the right threshold? Too high means legitimate completions get rejected; too low means false positives. (6) Check that conversational_action_completed events flow through the GameEventBus bridge at BabylonGame.ts ~line 3984 to QCE.trackConversationalAction().

tasks.title: Audit listen-and-repeat system — can the player actually listen, repeat, and pass?
tasks.description: Listen-and-repeat quests require the player to hear an NPC speak a phrase in the target language and then repeat it back, with pronunciation scoring. ListenAndRepeatAction (shared/game-engine/logic/actions/ListenAndRepeatAction.ts) and ListenAndRepeatController (shared/game-engine/rendering/ListenAndRepeatController.ts) exist but may not be fully wired. (1) Verify ListenAndRepeatController is initialized in BabylonGame.ts — search for its constructor call and callback wiring. If it's imported but never instantiated, wire it up. (2) Check the full flow: NPC speaks a phrase (via TTS through InsimulClient) → player hears it → player clicks a 'Repeat' button or presses a key → microphone captures speech (via STT through InsimulClient) → pronunciation is scored against the expected phrase → result determines pass/fail. (3) Verify audio capture: does the microphone actually work in-game? Check if InsimulClient.transcribeSpeech() is wired and functional. If STT is not available (browser mode without mic permissions), is there a fallback (e.g., type-to-repeat)? (4) Verify pronunciation scoring: ListenAndRepeatAction has a scorePronunciation() function with a pass threshold of 60. Is this scoring real (comparing phonemes) or stubbed? If stubbed, implement a simple Levenshtein-distance-based scoring between the expected text and the transcribed text. (5) Verify event emission: listen_and_repeat_passed events must flow to QuestCompletionEngine (line 237 handles this case). Check that the result from ListenAndRepeatController fires this event on the GameEventBus. (6) Check the chatPanel callback: BabylonGame.ts has chatPanel.setOnListenAndRepeat() at ~line 2864. Verify this callback processes the result and emits the correct event. (7) If audio STT is unavailable, add a text-input fallback: show the phrase, let the player type it back in the target language, score based on exact match / edit distance.

tasks.title: Audit conversational quest completion — does the LLM evaluation pipeline work end-to-end?
tasks.description: The conversation goal evaluation pipeline was recently built to allow LLM-based assessment of whether a player's conversation achieves quest objectives. Verify every link in the chain. (1) In BabylonChatPanel.ts, after each player message, requestMetadata() is called. Verify it includes activeObjectives from questBridge.getObjectivesForEvaluation(). Log the objectives being sent to confirm they're populated. (2) On the server in http-bridge.ts, verify the /api/conversation/metadata endpoint: does it receive the activeObjectives array? Does buildConversationGoalPrompt() construct a valid LLM prompt that asks the model to evaluate each objective against the conversation transcript? (3) Verify the LLM response parsing: the server should return goalEvaluations as an array of { questId, objectiveId, goalMet, confidence, extractedInfo }. Check that the response format is correct and consistently parseable. (4) Back in BabylonChatPanel.ts, verify questBridge.processEvaluations() is called with the server response. Log each evaluation to confirm they arrive. (5) Test with a real conversation: start a quest with a conversation objective (e.g., 'introduce yourself to the baker'), have the conversation in the target language, and verify the objective completes within 1-3 exchanges. If it never completes, check: are the objectives being sent? Is the server evaluating them? Is the confidence too low? (6) Verify grammar feedback emission: the recently-added grammar_feedback event (emitted when metadata.grammarFeedback.status === 'correct') should flow to QCE for grammar_demonstrated objectives. (7) Verify translation attempt emission: the recently-added translation_attempt events (emitted per vocabHint) should flow to QCE for translate_text objectives. (8) Verify friendship tracking: the recently-added npc_relationship_changed event (emitted on conversation start with per-NPC counter) should flow to QCE for build_friendship objectives.

tasks.title: Audit vocabulary system — spaced repetition, word tracking, and reward integration
tasks.description: The vocabulary system should introduce new words, track mastery, use spaced repetition, and reward progress. Multiple systems interact: HoverTranslationSystem, PersistentLanguageTracker, vocabulary hints from metadata, and vocabulary quest objectives. (1) Verify word introduction: when the player hovers over a target-language word in conversation (BabylonChatPanel hover-to-translate), in building signs (BuildingSignManager), or in texts (Library), the word should be added to their vocabulary as 'new'. Check if onVocabularyLookup fires and feeds into PersistentLanguageTracker. (2) Verify word tracking: PersistentLanguageTracker should track each word's mastery level (new → learning → familiar → mastered) based on correct usage count. Check if vocabHints from the metadata response update word mastery — each vocab hint should increment the usage count. (3) Verify spaced repetition: is there an SRS (spaced repetition system) that schedules word review? Check if the vocabulary system has review intervals, due dates, or a Leitner box implementation. If not, this is a gap — players need periodic word review prompts. (4) Verify vocabulary rewards: BabylonGame.ts has chatPanel.setOnNewWordLearned() and chatPanel.setOnWordMastered() callbacks (~line 2968-2973). Do these trigger XP awards, toast notifications, or other feedback? (5) Verify vocabulary quest objectives: use_vocabulary objectives should complete when the player uses specific words. Check that the vocabulary_used event bridge (BabylonGame.ts ~line 2834) flows to QCE.trackVocabularyUsage(). (6) Check if the vocabulary is accessible to the player: is there a 'Vocabulary' tab in the game menu that shows learned words, mastery levels, and translations? (7) Verify vocabulary persists across sessions: does saving/loading a game preserve the vocabulary state?

tasks.title: Audit tutorial system — can a new player complete the tutorial successfully?
tasks.description: New players should get a guided tutorial that teaches basic controls and leads into the assessment. TutorialQuestSystem (shared/game-engine/logic/TutorialQuestSystem.ts) defines tutorial objectives with step types (movement, camera, interaction, inventory, reading, quest_log, assessment_reading, assessment_listening, assessment_conversation) but may not be wired into the game. (1) Check if TutorialQuestSystem is instantiated in BabylonGame.ts. Search for its constructor call. If it's not instantiated, it's entirely non-functional. (2) Check what happens on first game load: does OnboardingLauncher (shared/game-engine/rendering/OnboardingLauncher.ts) detect a first playthrough and trigger the tutorial? What's the sequence: does the player see the tutorial quest first, then the assessment, then the main quest? (3) Map the tutorial steps: what should the player do? Expected flow: (a) WASD to move — show a HUD hint, detect player movement, mark complete. (b) Mouse to look — detect camera rotation. (c) Approach an NPC — detect proximity to any NPC. (d) Press Enter/G to talk — detect conversation opened. (e) Open inventory (I key) — detect inventory panel opened. (f) Open quest log (J key) — detect quest log opened. (g) Read a sign — detect building sign interaction. (h) Assessment begins. (4) Verify HUD hints: each tutorial step should show a persistent on-screen hint (e.g., 'Use WASD to move around') that disappears when the step is completed. Check if a HUD hint system exists. (5) Verify step completion detection: each step type needs an event listener or state check. Are these wired? (6) Verify the tutorial cannot be accidentally skipped or broken — what happens if the player wanders off?

tasks.title: Audit assessment system — can the initial language assessment be completed?
tasks.description: The initial assessment determines the player's CEFR level and gates quest difficulty. AssessmentEngine (shared/game-engine/logic/AssessmentEngine.ts) runs 4 phases: reading comprehension (15 pts), writing (15 pts), listening comprehension (13 pts), and conversation (10 pts). OnboardingLauncher triggers it on first playthrough. (1) Verify OnboardingLauncher detects first playthrough: check the API call that determines if the player has completed assessment. If it always returns 'completed' (stubbed), assessment never triggers. (2) Walk through each assessment phase: Phase 1 Reading — does the AssessmentEngine generate reading content at the appropriate level? Is the UI (AssessmentModalUI.ts) functional? Can the player read a passage and answer comprehension questions? Does scoring work? Phase 2 Writing — is there a text input where the player writes in the target language? Is scoring functional or stubbed? Phase 3 Listening — does audio play (via TTS)? Can the player listen and answer questions about what they heard? Phase 4 Conversation — does an NPC conversation happen? Is it scored? (3) Check stub status: the summary mentioned assessment calls were 'stubbed pending Prolog integration'. Verify which parts are stubbed (content generation? scoring? both?) and what the stubs return. If scoring is stubbed to always return a fixed score, the assessment is non-functional. (4) Verify CEFR level assignment: after all 4 phases, a total score should map to A1/A2/B1/B2. Check the scoring thresholds and confirm the result is stored. (5) Verify the CEFR level is used: quest difficulty, vocabulary level, and content gating should all reference the player's CEFR level. Check if getPlayerCefrLevel() is called anywhere in quest assignment or content generation. (6) Check the UI panels: AssessmentResultsPanel, AssessmentModalUI, AssessmentInstructionOverlay — do they render correctly? Are transitions between phases smooth?

tasks.title: Audit quest objective feasibility — flag any quest steps that cannot be completed in-game
tasks.description: Every quest objective across all quest types (guild quests, main quests, radiant quests) must be achievable through actual in-game actions. Run a systematic audit. (1) Load the full quest corpus: read data/seed/language/guild-quests.json, data/seed/language/seed-quests.json, and any quest generator outputs (crafting-quest-generator.ts, fetch-quest-generator.ts, side-quest-generator.ts). Catalog every unique objective type used. (2) Cross-reference each objective type against QuestCompletionEngine.ts switch cases (lines 280-455). Every objective type used in quests MUST have a corresponding handler. Flag any objective types that appear in quests but have no handler. (3) For each handler, verify the event bridge exists: trace from the handler back through the event bus to the actual gameplay action that emits the event. For example: craft_item handler ← item_crafted event ← CraftingSystem.completeCraft(). Flag any handlers that have no event source (dead handlers). (4) Check quest-world binding: objectives that reference specific NPCs (talk_to_npc with npcId), locations (visit_location with locationId), or items (collect_item with itemName) must reference entities that actually exist in the game world. Flag objectives with placeholder IDs like '{npcId}', '{locationId}', etc. that were never resolved during world generation. (5) Check quest-action mapping: shared/game-engine/quest-action-mapping.ts maps objective types to player actions. Verify every mapping points to a functional action. (6) Run the existing quest-completability-validator (shared/quest-completability-validator.ts) and quest-feasibility-validator (shared/quest-feasibility-validator.ts) against the test world and report results. (7) Produce a final report: for each unfeasible quest, recommend either fixing the objective (wiring the missing event/action) or discarding the quest.

tasks.title: Audit quest reward and progression flow — do completed quests give rewards and unlock next steps?
tasks.description: When a quest completes, the player should receive rewards (XP, gold, items, vocabulary, reputation) and the next quest in any chain should become available. (1) Verify quest completion detection: when all objectives in a quest are marked complete, QuestCompletionEngine should fire a quest_completed event. Check the allComplete check at ~line 472 and confirm the onQuestCompleted callback fires. (2) Verify reward distribution: check BabylonGame.ts quest completion handler. When quest_completed fires, does the player receive: XP (added to player level/progress), gold (added to playerGold), items (added to inventory), reputation (added to settlement standing)? Check each reward type's implementation. (3) Verify quest turn-in flow: for quests assigned by NPCs, does the player need to return to the quest-giver to turn in? Check chatPanel.setOnQuestTurnedIn() (~line 2777). Does the NPC acknowledge completion with dialogue? (4) Verify guild quest chain progression: when a guild quest completes, does GuildQuestManager advance the guild tier and make the next quest available? Check the wiring between quest completion events and GuildQuestManager.receiveNextQuest(). (5) Verify main quest chapter transitions: when all objectives in a main quest chapter complete, does the next chapter unlock? Check main-quest-progression.ts. (6) Check for soft-locks: is there any scenario where a quest becomes impossible to complete (NPC dies/disappears, item is consumed, location becomes inaccessible) without a way to abandon or reset it? (7) Verify the quest log updates: completed quests should move to a 'Completed' section. Active quests should show updated objective progress. Failed/abandoned quests should be clearly marked.

tasks.title: Audit building entry/exit and interior interaction — can the player navigate all buildings?
tasks.description: Players must be able to enter any building, interact with interior NPCs and objects, and exit reliably. Recent fixes added door click-to-exit but the full flow needs verification. (1) Verify building entry: can the player click on any building door (or press E near it) to enter? Check that all business types and residences have buildingType/buildingId metadata on their meshes and that the pointer handler at BabylonGame.ts ~line 9891 processes them. Test with: Shop, Bakery, Bar, Blacksmith, Church, Guild halls, residences. (2) Verify interior generation: does BuildingInteriorGenerator create a reasonable interior for each business type? Check that furniture, doors, and walls are placed correctly. Test for GuildArtisans specifically — does it have workbenches/crafting stations? (3) Verify interior NPC spawning: when the player enters a building, are the owner and employees placed inside? Check InteriorNPCManager population at BabylonGame.ts ~line 9306. Can the player interact with interior NPCs (click to select, Enter to chat, radial menu for actions)? (4) Verify exit works: the recently-added onExitCallback wiring should allow clicking the front entrance door to exit. Also verify E-key exit works. Also verify the exit_door frame (invisible clickable box) works. Test all three exit methods. (5) Verify containers and items in interiors: ContainerSpawnSystem should spawn loot containers (chests, barrels) inside buildings with context-appropriate items. Can the player interact with these? (6) Verify action hotspots in interiors: PlayerActionSystem.getHotspotsForBusiness() should register crafting/cooking/other hotspots at appropriate furniture. Do these work? (7) Check for getting stuck: can the player get trapped in any interior (no exit works, collision issues, camera stuck)? Test in several building types.

tasks.title: Audit minimap quest markers and waypoint guidance — can the player find quest objectives?
tasks.description: Players need clear guidance on where to go for quest objectives. The minimap should show markers for active quest targets, and waypoints should guide navigation. (1) Check QuestMinimapMarkers (shared/game-engine/logic/QuestMinimapMarkers.ts): does it generate marker positions for active quest objectives? Does it handle different objective types (visit_location → location marker, talk_to_npc → NPC marker, collect_item → item/container marker, craft_item → crafting station marker)? (2) Check DynamicQuestWaypointDirector (shared/game-engine/logic/DynamicQuestWaypointDirector.ts): does it create on-screen directional indicators pointing toward quest targets? (3) Check QuestWaypointManager (shared/game-engine/rendering/QuestWaypointManager.ts): does it render 3D waypoint markers in the world above quest-relevant locations/NPCs? (4) Verify integration: are these systems instantiated in BabylonGame.ts and updated each frame? Do markers appear on the minimap for the currently tracked quest? (5) Test with a new quest: accept a quest, open the map, verify markers appear at the correct locations. Follow the markers and verify they lead to the right places. (6) Check for quest objective types that can't be mapped: conversation objectives, vocabulary objectives, and grammar objectives don't have a physical location. These should either point to the relevant NPC or show a 'speak with anyone' indicator. (7) Verify marker cleanup: when a quest objective completes, its marker should disappear. When a quest is completed or abandoned, all its markers should be removed.

tasks.title: Write automated E2E quest completion smoke tests
tasks.description: Create a test suite that programmatically verifies quest objectives can be completed by simulating the minimum player actions required. This is the 'test of tests' that validates the entire quest pipeline. (1) Create shared/game-engine/__tests__/QuestE2ESmoke.test.ts. For each quest objective type in QuestCompletionEngine's switch statement (49 types), write a test that: creates a mock quest with that objective type, fires the corresponding event on a mock GameEventBus, and asserts the objective is marked complete. This validates the QCE handlers. (2) Create a bridge coverage test: for each GameEventBus event type that has a bridge in BabylonGame.ts (20+ bridges at lines 3829-3986), verify the bridge exists and forwards to the correct QCE event type. This validates the event flow. (3) Create a quest corpus feasibility test: load all guild quests from data/seed/language/guild-quests.json, all seed quests from seed-quests.json, parse every objective, and verify each objective type has: (a) a QCE handler, (b) an event bridge, (c) a gameplay action that can emit the event. Report coverage percentage. (4) Create a main quest walkability test: load the main quest chain, and for each objective in each chapter, verify the objective type is completable and the referenced NPCs/locations/items exist (or are template variables that get resolved). (5) Create a conversation objective test: verify that ConversationQuestBridge.getObjectivesForEvaluation() returns objectives for each conversation-type objective (talk_to_npc, introduce_self, ask_for_directions, order_food, etc.) and that processEvaluations() with a mock positive evaluation completes the objective. (6) Target: 100% of quest objective types should have passing smoke tests.

tasks.title: Audit radiant quest generation and assignment — do procedural side quests work?
tasks.description: Beyond guild quests and the main quest, NPCs should dynamically offer radiant (procedural) side quests. Multiple generators exist: crafting-quest-generator.ts, fetch-quest-generator.ts, side-quest-generator.ts, plus quest template systems. (1) Check if radiant quests are generated during world creation or on-the-fly during gameplay. Search for quest generator calls in the server startup/world creation flow and in BabylonGame.ts. (2) Check how radiant quests are assigned to NPCs: does each NPC have a pool of quests they can offer? Is this populated from the quest generators? Or do NPCs only offer quests that were pre-seeded in the database? (3) Verify quest offering during conversation: when the player talks to an NPC who has an available quest, does the NPC mention it? Check the quest guidance system (fetchQuestGuidance in BabylonGame.ts ~line 13004) — does it inject quest context into the NPC's system prompt so they naturally bring up the quest? (4) Check quest variety: are generated quests diverse enough? A player shouldn't get 5 'collect 3 herbs' quests in a row. Check if generators have anti-repetition logic. (5) Verify quest level scaling: radiant quests should match the player's CEFR level. Check if generators use the player's level to select appropriate vocabulary, conversation complexity, and objective types. (6) Check quest limits: is there a cap on simultaneous active side quests? Players shouldn't be overwhelmed with 20 active quests. (7) Verify radiant quests are completable: since they're procedurally generated, their objectives might reference items/NPCs/locations that don't exist. Run the quest-feasibility-validator against generated quests.

tasks.title: Audit NPC daily schedules — do NPCs follow complete, visible daily routines?
tasks.description: NPCs must follow believable daily routines that the player can observe: wake up at home, commute to work, work all day, run errands, socialize, go home, sleep. ScheduleExecutor (shared/game-engine/rendering/ScheduleExecutor.ts, ~1025 lines) manages the daily cycle with personality-driven wake/sleep times, and NPCScheduleSystem (shared/game-engine/rendering/NPCScheduleSystem.ts, ~900 lines) picks goals using Big Five personality traits. The system has occasion types: sleeping, working, commuting, errand, visiting, leisure, home, idle. (1) Verify wake-up in home interiors: ScheduleExecutor lines 346-349 check (state.isInsideBuilding && state.occasion === 'sleeping') and call handleWakeUp(). Does this actually transition the NPC from a sleeping animation inside their home to standing and walking out the door? If the player is inside the same home, do they see the NPC wake up and leave? Check that the NPC's home has a bed assignment (InteriorLayout.beds) and that they spawn there during sleep hours. (2) Verify morning routine: after waking, does the NPC idle briefly at the door (ScheduleExecutor lines 963-965 mention 2-3 seconds) before commuting? Is there any visible morning activity (eating, getting ready) or does the NPC just teleport outside? If there's no morning routine, add a brief idle at home furniture (table for 30-60 game seconds) before departing. (3) Verify work commute: ScheduleExecutor detects isMorningCommute (line 463-464) and paths via findSidewalkPathForNPC() (line 487). Does the NPC actually walk along sidewalks from home to work? Do they enter the work building through the door? Check that building entry is visible (not teleport). (4) Verify NPC greetings en route: NPCScheduleSystem checks for social interrupts when two NPCs pass within proximity. When NPCs cross paths on sidewalks, do they play greeting animations? Does the AmbientConversationSystem trigger NPC-to-NPC conversations during commutes? (5) Verify work behavior: during work hours, does the NPC stay inside their workplace? Check BusinessBehaviorSystem — are they performing occupation-specific animations (baker kneading, blacksmith hammering) or just standing idle? If the player enters the business, is the NPC visible and interactable? (6) Verify unemployed NPCs get guild assignments: NPCScheduleSystem line 655 has an unemployed schedule. If an NPC has no workplace, are they assigned to a guild as their 'workplace'? If not, add logic to assign unemployed NPCs to the nearest guild so they have somewhere to go during work hours. (7) Verify post-work evening: pickEveningGoal() at line 611 routes NPCs to social venues (extroverts), friend visits (agreeable), exploration (open), or home (introverts). Does this actually happen? Do NPCs go to the restaurant/bar/park after work? Are they visible there? (8) Verify bedtime: sendHome() at line 516 is called at the personality-driven bedtime (20:00-23:00). Does the NPC walk home, enter the building, and transition to sleeping state? Check that the sleep animation plays if the player enters the home at night. (9) End-to-end test: observe a single NPC for an entire game day cycle (24 game hours) and verify they complete the full routine: sleep → wake → commute → work → lunch break → work → evening activity → go home → sleep.

tasks.title: Audit NPC pathfinding — do NPCs navigate without getting stuck on walls, porches, and obstacles?
tasks.description: NPCs must navigate the settlement reliably without getting stuck on walls, trapped in porches, or walking through obstacles. PathfindingSystem (shared/game-engine/rendering/PathfindingSystem.ts, ~531 lines) wraps NavigationSystem with dynamic obstacles, and NPCMovementController (shared/game-engine/rendering/NPCMovementController.ts, ~469 lines) handles waypoint following and steering. (1) Verify wall avoidance: when an NPC's path takes them near a building wall, do they steer around it or walk into it and get stuck? PathfindingSystem has getAvoidanceOffset() (line 246) that computes lateral steering perpendicular to heading when obstacles are ahead (dot product filter at line 270). Check if this avoidance force is strong enough to prevent wall collisions. Test by observing NPCs walking near building corners — do they smoothly round the corner or clip through/get stuck? If NPCs frequently get stuck, increase the avoidance force magnitude or add a wall-slide recovery that detects zero-velocity-with-active-goal and nudges the NPC perpendicular to the wall normal. (2) Verify obstacle collision handling: NPCMovementController applies avoidance offsets at lines 273-278. Are building meshes, fences, carts, market stalls, and other world objects registered as obstacles? Check if ProceduralBuildingGenerator and OutdoorFurnitureGenerator mark their meshes with checkCollisions=true or register them with PathfindingSystem. If not, NPCs will walk through them. (3) Verify porch spawning: when NPCs spawn or are assigned idle positions near buildings, do they end up on top of porches and get stuck? Porches have steps and raised platforms (BuildingInteriorGenerator hasPorch, porchDepth, porchSteps). Check if NPC spawn position selection in NPCScheduleSystem avoids porch areas — sidewalk nodes should be offset far enough from buildings (halfWidth + 1 at line 184) to clear porches. If NPCs do spawn on porches, add a porch exclusion zone to spawn point selection (check building metadata for hasPorch and expand the exclusion radius by porchDepth). (4) Verify door-finding: when an NPC needs to enter a building (for work or home), do they pathfind to the building's door position? ScheduleExecutor uses computeDoorPosition() (line 81). Verify this returns the correct position (front face of building, at ground level, centered on the door). Check that NPCs don't try to enter through walls or the back of the building. (5) Verify exit pathfinding: when an NPC inside a building needs to leave, do they find the interior door and exit cleanly? Or do they get stuck walking against interior walls trying to reach the door? Check if interior NPCs use the InteriorLayout.doorPosition for exit pathfinding. (6) Verify stuck detection and recovery: ScheduleExecutor has abandonGoal() at line 848. What triggers it? Is there a timeout (e.g., if NPC hasn't moved more than 0.5 units in 5 seconds, abandon goal and pick a new one)? Check BabylonGame.ts updateSingleNPCBehavior() for stuck detection logic. If no stuck detection exists, add one: track each NPC's position each second, and if they've moved less than 0.3 units in 5 consecutive checks while having an active movement goal, trigger a recovery (teleport to nearest sidewalk node, clear path, pick new goal). (7) Verify path re-computation: PathfindingSystem validates active paths every 500ms (line 411-453). When a path becomes blocked (new obstacle placed, another NPC blocking), does it re-request a path? Or does the NPC keep walking into the obstacle?

tasks.title: Audit NPC conversation facing — do NPCs turn to face conversation partners?
tasks.description: When a conversation starts between the player and an NPC (or between two NPCs), both participants must turn to face each other and maintain that facing for the duration of the conversation. NPCMovementController (shared/game-engine/rendering/NPCMovementController.ts) has startConversation(partnerPosition) at line 148 which saves the current movement command, stops movement, and sets a faceTarget. The update loop calls updateFaceTarget() (line 460-467) which computes targetAngle = Math.atan2(dx, dz) and smoothly rotates via smoothRotateToward() (line 365-390) with TURN_SPEED = 5.0 rad/s and a threshold of 0.05 rad (2.9°). NPCAnimationController has setConversationTarget() (line 212) and faceTarget() (lines 413-429) for animation-state-driven facing. (1) Verify player→NPC facing: when the player opens a conversation with an NPC (click to select, Enter/G to chat), does the NPC immediately begin turning to face the player? Check that BabylonGame.ts calls startConversation() on the NPC's movement controller when the chat panel opens. If not, add this call in the handleOpenChat() flow, passing the player's current position as the partner position. (2) Verify facing is maintained during conversation: as the player moves during a conversation (if allowed), does the NPC continuously update their facing to track the player position? Check if updateFaceTarget() is called each frame during the conversation state. The faceTarget should be the live player position, not a stale snapshot from when the conversation started. (3) Verify NPC→NPC facing for ambient conversations: when two NPCs have an ambient conversation (AmbientConversationSystem / NPCSocialInteractionSystem), do BOTH NPCs face each other? Check if both NPCs' movement controllers get startConversation() called with each other's positions. If only one NPC faces the other (or neither faces), wire both controllers. (4) Verify conversation end cleanup: when the conversation ends (player closes chat panel, NPC walks away), does endConversation() (line 168-180) get called? Does it clear faceTarget and restore the previous movement command? Check that NPCs resume their scheduled path after conversation, not just stand still. (5) Verify the NPC doesn't snap instantly: smoothRotateToward() uses lerp-based interpolation. Verify the turn looks natural (0.5-1 second to fully face the partner) and doesn't snap 180° in a single frame. Test with an NPC facing away from the player when conversation starts. (6) Verify NPC-to-NPC eavesdrop facing: when the player eavesdrops on an NPC-to-NPC conversation (BabylonChatPanel eavesdrop mode), the two NPCs should face each other, not the player. The player observes from outside. Check that the eavesdrop mode doesn't redirect NPC facing toward the player. (7) Verify facial animation sync: NPCAnimationController's conversation facing (lines 381-385) should play 'talk' and 'listen' animations that alternate between speakers. Check if these animations are triggered during conversation turns or if the NPC just plays a generic idle animation while facing.
